title: String Operators & Precedence
description: Concatenation, assignment with string, and understanding operator order.

slides.title: String Operators
slides.content:
# Joining Strings

There are two string operators:

*   `.`  – Concatenation operator. Returns the concatenation of its left and right arguments.
*   `.=` – Concatenation assignment. Appends the right argument to the left variable.

```php
<?php
$hello = "Hello ";
$world = "World";
$greeting = $hello . $world; // "Hello World"

$hello .= $world; // $hello now "Hello World" (same as $hello = $hello . $world)
?>
```

slides.title: Concatenation in Action
Operator Precedence
slides.content:
# Which Operator Goes First?

When an expression has multiple operators, PHP follows a set of rules called **operator precedence**.

For example:

```php
<?php
$result = 2 + 3 * 4; // 14, not 20, because * has higher precedence than +
?>
```

If operators have the same precedence, **associativity** (left-to-right or right-to-left) determines the order.

slides.title: Precedence Table (Simplified)
slides.content:
# Common Precedence (Highest to Lowest)

1.  `++` `--` (increment/decrement)
2.  `!` (logical NOT)
3.  `*` `/` `%`
4.  `+` `-` `.`
5.  `<` `<=` `>` `>=`
6.  `==` `!=` `===` `!==` `<>` `<=>`
7.  `&&`
8.  `||`
9.  `=` `+=` `.=` etc. (assignment)
10. `and` `or` `xor`

Use parentheses `()` to force your own order!

slides.title: Using Parentheses
slides.content:
```php
<?php
// Without parentheses
$result1 = 2 + 3 * 4;    // 14 (3*4 first)

// With parentheses
$result2 = (2 + 3) * 4;  // 20

// Logical operators and precedence
$bool = true && false || true; // (true && false) || true -> false || true -> true
$bool2 = true and false || true; // careful: 'and' has lower precedence than '=', so this is (true and false) || true? Actually 'and' has very low precedence, so it's evaluated after assignment. Better to always use && and ||.
?>
```

quizData.explanation: `*` has higher precedence than `+=`, so 3 * 2 = 6, then `$x += 6` makes 5+6=11.
